title: WITHDRAWAL of LIFE-SUSTAINING TREATMENT and COMPASSIONATE TERMINAL EXTUBATION in the ICU for a 78-Year-Old Man on Day 8 of Refractory Septic Shock with Multi-Organ Failure (Mechanical Ventilation + Vasopressors + CRRT) After an Interdisciplinary Family Meeting Confirms Continued Aggressive Care Is No Longer Aligned with His Documented Values: SURROGATE SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT by His Wife (DPOA-HC) Per His Advance Directive, the DOUBLE-EFFECT Principle (Aggressive Opioid + Benzodiazepine + Antimuscarinic Comfort Titration Is NOT Euthanasia), a Structured Compassionate Withdrawal Protocol (Pre-Medicate → Discontinue Vasopressors and Devices → REMOVE the Ventilator → Continue Comfort Titration) with FAMILY PRESENCE, and Bereavement + Team Support
subtitle: WITHDRAWAL of life-sustaining treatment is a routine but profoundly important ICU practice when continued aggressive care no longer aligns with the patient's values or goals — distinct from EUTHANASIA. The patient is dying of his illness; the team is removing interventions that are no longer beneficial and providing intensive COMFORT care. This case is about the ETHICAL and PROCEDURAL framework: confirming the decision (goals-of-care reassessment with the surrogate, anchored in the patient's advance directive and known values), executing a COMPASSIONATE WITHDRAWAL protocol (pre-medicating with opioid + benzodiazepine ± antimuscarinic, discontinuing non-comfort interventions, REMOVING the ventilator, and titrating to comfort throughout), and supporting the family and team. It is distinct from non-ICU hospice (case-082), non-ICU acute goals-of-care conflict (case-092), brain death and organ donation (case-107 — the patient is already dead by neurologic criteria), and outpatient proactive advance care planning (case-142). DECISION-MAKING anchors in the patient's PRIOR EXPRESSED VALUES: an executed ADVANCE DIRECTIVE plus a DPOA-HC who applies SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what the PATIENT would want, not what the surrogate would prefer). When prior wishes are unknown, the standard is BEST INTEREST. Treating teams may declare an intervention non-beneficial when the clinical evidence shows it cannot achieve the patient's stated goals; conflict is managed by clarifying goals, time-limited trials, ethics consultation, and (rarely) institutional dispute-resolution policies. WITHDRAWAL is permissible at the patient's or surrogate's informed request — competent adults may refuse any treatment (links case-122) and surrogates may refuse on the basis of the patient's known wishes (links case-132). The DOUBLE-EFFECT PRINCIPLE governs aggressive symptom management at the end of life: an act that has a foreseeable but unintended harmful effect (e.g., respiratory depression from opioids) is ethically permissible when (1) the act itself is morally neutral or good, (2) the intent is the good effect (comfort), (3) the good effect is not achieved through the bad effect, and (4) there is proportionate reason. Opioids and benzodiazepines used to relieve dyspnea, pain, and anxiety at the end of life are NOT euthanasia — euthanasia is the intentional act of causing death, which is not what is being done here. SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT is the active work: OPIOID (morphine or fentanyl) titrated to relieve dyspnea and pain; BENZODIAZEPINE (midazolam, lorazepam) for anxiety/agitation; ANTIMUSCARINIC (glycopyrrolate or scopolamine) for terminal SECRETIONS (the 'death rattle') while educating the family that the rattle is not distressing to the patient; antiemetics if needed; minimize alarms and unnecessary stimuli. The COMPASSIONATE WITHDRAWAL PROTOCOL: (1) confirm decision and document orders (comfort-focused: DNAR; discontinue ventilator, vasopressors, dialysis, blood draws, monitoring devices not needed for comfort; suspend artificial nutrition/hydration per the patient's wishes), (2) prepare the room (lights soft, alarms off where safe, minimize equipment, position the patient comfortably, allow family at the bedside, chaplaincy as requested), (3) PRE-MEDICATE adequately (a generous opioid + benzodiazepine bolus before any maneuver to reduce dyspnea and air hunger), (4) DISCONTINUE the vasopressors and other non-comfort interventions (typically at the same time as withdrawal — they are not slowly titrated down because the goal is comfort, not survival), (5) REMOVE the ventilator (extubation if not contraindicated by airway/secretion concerns; otherwise convert to flow-by oxygen for visible comfort while withdrawing pressure support) and (6) CONTINUE titration to comfort — assessing dyspnea (use of accessory muscles, grimacing, restlessness), pain, secretions, agitation, and family-perceived distress; reassess and re-medicate as often as needed. TIME-TO-DEATH varies (minutes to hours to occasionally days) and is shaped by the underlying physiology — share this honestly with the family. FAMILY presence is supported: explain what to expect (irregular and slowing breathing, possible agonal respirations, color changes, secretions, transient eye opening, sometimes a brief 'rally'), invite them to speak, touch, play music, and be present in their way; offer chaplaincy and bereavement resources. After death: pronounce per policy; allow family time at the bedside; offer mortuary/funeral information; complete documentation; conduct a TEAM DEBRIEF — moral distress in ICU teams during withdrawal is real and supporting each other is part of the work. KEY DISTINCTIONS: WITHDRAWAL/WITHHOLDING are ethically equivalent (you may withhold an intervention you could also stop). PHYSICIAN-ASSISTED DEATH/MEDICAL AID IN DYING is jurisdiction-specific and is NOT what is happening here. DNAR addresses only CPR; comfort care continues across other interventions. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: equating compassionate symptom titration with euthanasia; under-medicating because of fear of 'hastening' death; abruptly extubating without adequate pre-medication; failing to discontinue non-comfort interventions (continuing labs, pressors, dialysis after the goal has changed); over-disclosing or imposing values; describing comfort medications as 'a lethal injection'; failing to support the family or team. NURSING ROLE: anchor decisions in the patient's documented values via the surrogate; coordinate the interdisciplinary plan (ICU, palliative care, chaplaincy, social work, ethics if needed); pre-medicate, reassess, and titrate aggressively to COMFORT under the double-effect principle (this is not euthanasia); execute the structured withdrawal protocol; support family presence with honest, gentle anticipatory guidance; document objectively; lead the team debrief afterward.

patient.name: J. Whitford
patient.code: DNAR per surrogate decision today, anchored in the patient's executed advance directive and substituted judgment; goal = compassionate withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment with intensive COMFORT care — anchor the decision in the patient's values, coordinate the interdisciplinary plan, pre-medicate aggressively to comfort under the double-effect principle (NOT euthanasia), execute a structured withdrawal protocol (discontinue non-comfort interventions, remove the ventilator, continue titration to comfort), support family presence with honest anticipatory guidance, document objectively, and lead a team debrief
patient.admitDate: ICU day 8 for refractory septic shock from urosepsis with multi-organ failure: mechanical ventilation, dual vasopressors (norepinephrine + vasopressin) escalating, CRRT for AKI/hyperkalemia, transaminitis, hypotensive on max therapy, encephalopathic. PMH: HFrEF (LVEF 25%), CKD stage 4 pre-ICU, COPD GOLD III on home O2, T2DM, prior stroke with mild aphasia (baseline). Executed advance directive on file: 'If treatment becomes a burden without a meaningful benefit, I want comfort only and no prolonged life support.' Wife B. is the documented DPOA-HC; adult daughter and son in agreement. After serial discussions with the ICU + palliative-care team and a formal family meeting today, the surrogate decides — applying SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT — to TRANSITION TO COMFORT CARE with compassionate withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment. Plan: structured withdrawal protocol with intensive symptom management and family presence.
patient.room: Medical ICU; private room arranged; lights soft; alarms minimized; family at the bedside; interdisciplinary team (intensivist, ICU RN, respiratory therapist, palliative-care MD/NP, chaplaincy, social work, ethics on standby, bereavement coordinator)

vitals.temp: 37.9°C; refractory shock + multi-organ failure; documented advance directive + DPOA-HC; family meeting with palliative care concludes — continued aggressive care is no longer aligned with the patient's stated values; substituted judgment → COMFORT CARE; orders updated (DNAR + discontinue non-comfort interventions); plan for compassionate withdrawal today

37.9°C; room prepared — soft lights, alarms set to minimum/safe, unnecessary equipment removed; family present; chaplain offers a prayer; nurse explains what to expect (variable time to death, irregular breathing, possible secretions, color changes, occasional brief 'rally'); CRRT discontinued; labs stopped; suspended artificial nutrition/hydration per the directive; transition to comfort orders verified

37.8°C; OPIOID (morphine or fentanyl) bolus + BENZODIAZEPINE (midazolam or lorazepam) bolus given for dyspnea/pain/anxiety BEFORE any maneuver; antimuscarinic (glycopyrrolate) ordered for terminal secretions; doses titrated to comfort (relaxed face, no accessory-muscle use, calm), intent = comfort (the principle of DOUBLE EFFECT — relief of suffering, NOT euthanasia)

vitals.spo2: Decreasing — comfort, not numbers, drives the plan
vitals.temp: 37.6°C; vasopressors and remaining non-comfort interventions DISCONTINUED (the goal is comfort, not survival — they are not slowly titrated down); ventilator REMOVED (extubated to room air or flow-by O2); continuous bedside presence; opioid/benzodiazepine re-bolused for any signs of dyspnea/restlessness; family at the bedside, encouraged to talk, touch, and play music

vitals.spo2: Decreasing — monitor removed; focus on comfort signs
vitals.temp: 37.4°C; continuous comfort assessment — face relaxed, no accessory-muscle use, no grimacing/restlessness; opioid + benzodiazepine titrated as needed; glycopyrrolate given for secretions (educate family the 'rattle' does not distress the patient); chaplain at bedside; brief 'rally' acknowledged calmly; minimize alarms and unnecessary stimuli; family is invited to be present in their own way

Cooling; death pronounced per policy; family given private time at the bedside; mortuary/funeral information offered; bereavement coordinator engaged; objective documentation completed (decision-making + medication doses/times + procedure timeline + family interactions); TEAM DEBRIEF held — moral distress acknowledged, chaplaincy/peer support offered

labs.n: PRESENTATION + decision-making framework (advance directive + surrogate substituted judgment; not euthanasia)
labs.v: 78 y/o man, ICU day 8 for refractory septic shock with multi-organ failure (mechanical ventilation + dual vasopressors at maximum + CRRT for AKI/hyperK + encephalopathy + transaminitis); aggressive interventions are no longer achieving the patient's stated goals. An EXECUTED ADVANCE DIRECTIVE specifies 'no prolonged life support if no meaningful recovery,' and his WIFE is the documented DPOA-HC. After an interdisciplinary family meeting (intensivist + palliative care + nurse + chaplain), the wife applies SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what HE would want, anchored in the directive and his values) and decides to TRANSITION to COMFORT CARE with WITHDRAWAL of life-sustaining treatment. This is ETHICALLY DISTINCT from euthanasia: the patient is dying of his illness, and the team is REMOVING non-beneficial interventions and providing intensive COMFORT care — NOT intentionally causing death. WITHHOLDING and WITHDRAWING are ethically equivalent (an intervention that could be withheld may also be withdrawn). When prior wishes are unknown, the standard is BEST INTEREST. DNAR addresses only CPR; comfort care continues across other interventions. Conflict is addressed by clarifying goals, time-limited trials, ethics consultation, and (rarely) institutional dispute-resolution policies. This case is distinct from non-ICU hospice ([[case-082]]), non-ICU acute goals-of-care family conflict ([[case-092]]), brain death and organ donation ([[case-107]] — already dead by neurologic criteria), and outpatient proactive ACP ([[case-142]]).
labs.r: Anchor in the patient's PRIOR VALUES: executed AD + DPOA-HC applying SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what HE would want); BEST INTEREST when wishes unknown. WITHHOLDING/WITHDRAWING are ethically equivalent. WITHDRAWAL of non-beneficial therapy + intensive COMFORT = NOT EUTHANASIA (he is dying of his illness; team removes non-beneficial interventions and provides comfort — not intentionally causing death). DNAR = only CPR. Conflict → clarify goals + time-limited trials + ethics + institutional policy. RN role: anchor decisions in values; coordinate the team.

labs.n: DOUBLE-EFFECT principle + symptom management at end of life
labs.v: The DOUBLE-EFFECT principle governs aggressive symptom management at the end of life: an act with a foreseeable but UNINTENDED harmful effect (e.g., respiratory depression from opioids) is ETHICALLY PERMISSIBLE when (1) the act is morally neutral or good, (2) the INTENT is the good effect (comfort), (3) the good effect is NOT achieved through the bad effect, and (4) there is PROPORTIONATE reason. Opioids and benzodiazepines used to relieve dyspnea, pain, and anxiety at the end of life are NOT euthanasia. Under-medicating because of fear of 'hastening' death is a common, harmful error. SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT for compassionate withdrawal: OPIOID (morphine or fentanyl) titrated to relieve DYSPNEA and PAIN (assess by face, accessory-muscle use, grimacing, restlessness — not by an arbitrary respiratory rate cutoff); BENZODIAZEPINE (midazolam, lorazepam) for ANXIETY/AGITATION (especially before withdrawal of vent support); ANTIMUSCARINIC (glycopyrrolate IV or scopolamine patch) for terminal SECRETIONS (the 'death rattle' — educate the family it does NOT distress the patient); antiemetics if needed; antipyretic and skin/mouth care for comfort; minimize alarms and unnecessary stimuli. Re-bolus and titrate AS OFTEN AS NEEDED — there is no 'maximum dose' when the goal is comfort and the patient is dying. Document the doses and indications objectively.
labs.r: DOUBLE-EFFECT: act morally neutral/good + INTENT = comfort + good effect NOT achieved through bad effect + proportionate reason. Opioids + benzodiazepines for EOL symptoms = NOT euthanasia. Under-medicating from fear of 'hastening' death is harmful. SYMPTOMS: OPIOID (morphine/fentanyl) for dyspnea/pain; BENZO (midazolam/lorazepam) for anxiety/agitation; ANTIMUSCARINIC (glycopyrrolate/scopolamine) for secretions ('death rattle' — educate family it doesn't distress pt); antiemetics + skin/mouth care; minimize alarms. Titrate to comfort; no max dose at EOL. RN role: aggressive comfort under double-effect.

labs.n: Structured compassionate withdrawal protocol + family presence + after-death care
labs.v: The COMPASSIONATE WITHDRAWAL PROTOCOL is choreographed: (1) confirm the decision and document COMFORT-FOCUSED orders — DNAR; discontinue ventilator/vasopressors/dialysis/blood draws/non-comfort devices; suspend artificial nutrition/hydration per the patient's wishes; transition all monitoring to comfort assessment; (2) PREPARE the room — soft lights, alarms minimized/silenced where safe, unnecessary equipment removed, patient positioned comfortably, family at the bedside, chaplaincy as requested; (3) PRE-MEDICATE adequately with an opioid + benzodiazepine BOLUS BEFORE any maneuver (the most under-done step in many withdrawals); (4) DISCONTINUE vasopressors and remaining non-comfort interventions (typically at the SAME TIME as withdrawal — they are not slowly titrated down because the goal is comfort, not survival); (5) REMOVE the ventilator (extubation when feasible; otherwise convert to flow-by O2 for visible comfort while withdrawing pressure support); (6) CONTINUE titration to comfort — reassess every few minutes and re-bolus opioid/benzodiazepine for dyspnea/grimacing/restlessness, glycopyrrolate for secretions, and reposition/mouth care/skin care; (7) FAMILY presence is supported throughout — explain what to expect (irregular and slowing breathing, possible agonal respirations, color changes, secretions, transient eye opening, occasional brief 'rally'); invite them to speak, touch, play music, and be present in their way; (8) AFTER DEATH — pronounce per policy; allow private time at the bedside; mortuary/funeral information; bereavement coordinator engaged; document objectively (decision-making, medications/doses/times, procedure timeline, family interactions); (9) TEAM DEBRIEF — moral distress in ICU teams during withdrawal is real and supporting each other is part of the work; chaplaincy/peer support offered. TIME-TO-DEATH is variable (minutes to hours, occasionally days) and is shaped by the underlying physiology — share this HONESTLY with the family.
labs.r: PROTOCOL: (1) confirm + document comfort orders (DNAR + d/c vent/pressors/dialysis/non-comfort + suspend ANH per wishes); (2) prepare room (soft lights, alarms min, equipment cleared, family + chaplain); (3) PRE-MEDICATE adequately (opioid + benzo BOLUS before any maneuver — most under-done step); (4) DISCONTINUE pressors + non-comfort (NOT slowly titrated — goal is comfort); (5) REMOVE ventilator (extubation when feasible, otherwise flow-by O2); (6) CONTINUE comfort titration q few min — opioid/benzo for dyspnea/grimace/restless, glyco for secretions, reposition/mouth/skin care; (7) FAMILY presence + honest expectations (irregular breathing, agonal, color, 'rally'); (8) AFTER DEATH — pronounce + private time + mortuary/bereavement + objective doc; (9) TEAM DEBRIEF. Time to death variable (min-hours-days); honest sharing.

labs.n: Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors + bereavement + team support
labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the team did NOT equate compassionate symptom titration with euthanasia; did NOT under-medicate from fear of 'hastening' death; did NOT abruptly extubate without adequate pre-medication; did NOT slowly titrate down vasopressors as if survival were the goal; did NOT continue CRRT/labs/non-comfort interventions after the goals had changed; did NOT describe comfort medications to the family as 'a lethal injection'; did NOT impose the team's values on the surrogate; did NOT proceed without documenting the surrogate's substituted-judgment rationale; did NOT skip the family preparation about what to expect; and did NOT neglect family bereavement or team debrief. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: an executed advance directive + a DPOA-HC applying substituted judgment; a formal interdisciplinary family meeting documented objectively; comfort-focused orders (DNAR + discontinuation of non-comfort interventions); adequate PRE-MEDICATION (opioid + benzodiazepine bolus) BEFORE any maneuver; structured withdrawal sequence (discontinue vasopressors and remaining non-comfort interventions → remove ventilator → continue titration); aggressive symptom assessment and titration (opioid for dyspnea/pain, benzodiazepine for anxiety, antimuscarinic for secretions, mouth/skin care); family presence with honest, gentle anticipatory guidance; chaplaincy and bereavement support; after-death care with private time and mortuary/funeral resources; and a team DEBRIEF acknowledging moral distress. EXPECTED outcome: the patient dies comfortably with his family present, his values honored, and his team supported.
labs.r: Negatives confirm correct practice (didn't equate comfort titration w/ euthanasia, didn't under-medicate, didn't extubate w/o pre-med, didn't slow-titrate pressors as if survival were goal, didn't continue non-comfort interventions, didn't say 'lethal injection,' didn't impose values, didn't proceed without surrogate rationale documented, didn't skip family prep, didn't skip bereavement/debrief). Protective: AD + DPOA-HC + substituted judgment + documented family meeting + comfort orders + pre-med opioid+benzo + structured protocol + aggressive symptom mgmt + family presence w/ honest anticipatory guidance + chaplaincy + bereavement + after-death private time + team DEBRIEF.

labs.v: 8.6 mmol/L (severe tissue hypoperfusion; refractory shock; not retreated after goals changed)
0.6 mcg/kg/min (max) plus vasopressin 0.04 units/min — MAP not maintained despite maximal therapy
labs.r: Variable; this is at the upper limit of usual ICU titration

labs.n: Pre-medication: morphine + midazolam bolus before ventilator removal
labs.v: Morphine 4-6 mg IV + midazolam 1-2 mg IV given as a comfort bolus before withdrawal; titrate every few minutes to relaxed face/no accessory-muscle use/calm — there is no 'maximum dose' when the goal is comfort and the patient is dying (double-effect principle)
labs.r: Doses are titrated to comfort, not to a target number

labs.v: Glycopyrrolate 0.2 mg IV given for 'death rattle' secretions; family taught that secretions do NOT distress the patient

nursesNote: Medical ICU day 8 — 78 y/o man, refractory septic shock w/ multi-organ failure (mech vent + max norepinephrine + vasopressin + CRRT + encephalopathic + transaminitis + lactate 8.6); aggressive care no longer aligned w/ his stated values per his EXECUTED advance directive ('no prolonged life support if no meaningful recovery'). Wife is documented DPOA-HC; adult daughter + son in agreement. INTERDISCIPLINARY family meeting (intensivist + palliative care + nurse + chaplain) — wife applies SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT and chooses TRANSITION to COMFORT CARE w/ WITHDRAWAL of life-sustaining treatment. Ethically distinct from euthanasia: he is dying of his illness; team removes non-beneficial interventions + delivers intensive COMFORT under the DOUBLE-EFFECT principle. Orders updated: DNAR; d/c ventilator/vasopressors/CRRT/labs/non-comfort devices; suspend artificial nutrition/hydration per the directive; comfort-focused medications. PREPARATION: private room, soft lights, alarms minimized/silenced where safe, unnecessary equipment removed, patient positioned comfortably, family at bedside, chaplain at bedside, plan explained honestly (variable time to death; irregular/slowing breaths; possible agonal respirations + secretions + color changes + transient eye opening + occasional 'rally'). PRE-MEDICATE adequately BEFORE any maneuver (most under-done step) — MORPHINE bolus + MIDAZOLAM bolus for dyspnea/pain/anxiety; GLYCOPYRROLATE for secretions; ANTIPYRETIC + mouth care; no max dose when goal is comfort. DISCONTINUE vasopressors + remaining non-comfort interventions (NOT slowly titrated — goal is comfort, not survival). REMOVE the ventilator (extubated). CONTINUE comfort titration q few min — reassess face/accessory-muscle use/grimace/restlessness/secretions; re-bolus opioid/benzo prn; reposition/mouth care/skin care. FAMILY presence supported throughout — encouraged to speak, touch, play music; chaplain prayed; brief 'rally' acknowledged calmly. Death pronounced per policy. Private time at bedside; mortuary/funeral information offered; bereavement coordinator engaged. Objective DOCUMENTATION (substituted-judgment rationale + family-meeting summary + every medication w/ dose/time/indication + procedure timeline + family interactions). TEAM DEBRIEF held — moral distress acknowledged + chaplaincy/peer support offered. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT equate comfort titration w/ euthanasia, did NOT under-medicate from fear of 'hastening' death, did NOT abruptly extubate w/o pre-med, did NOT slow-titrate pressors as if survival were the goal, did NOT continue CRRT/labs/non-comfort interventions, did NOT label comfort meds 'a lethal injection,' did NOT impose values on the surrogate, did NOT skip family anticipatory guidance, did NOT skip bereavement/debrief.
providerOrders: GOALS OF CARE: transition to COMFORT CARE per the surrogate's substituted-judgment decision anchored in the patient's executed advance directive; document the family meeting and rationale

DISCONTINUE: ventilator (extubation), vasopressors (norepinephrine + vasopressin), CRRT, routine blood draws and imaging, non-comfort monitors/devices; suspend artificial nutrition and hydration per the patient's wishes; minimize alarms

SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT (DOUBLE-EFFECT principle — comfort, NOT euthanasia): morphine IV bolus and infusion for dyspnea and pain titrated to comfort; midazolam IV bolus and infusion for anxiety and agitation; glycopyrrolate IV (or scopolamine patch) for terminal secretions; antiemetic if needed; pre-medicate adequately BEFORE any maneuver (extubation, repositioning); reassess and re-titrate every few minutes to relaxed face/no accessory-muscle use/calm — there is no 'maximum dose' at end of life

ROOM/FAMILY: private room; soft lights; alarms minimized where safe; remove unnecessary equipment; allow family at the bedside; chaplaincy as requested; honest anticipatory guidance (variable time to death; irregular and slowing breathing; possible agonal respirations; color changes; secretions; possible brief 'rally')

WITHDRAWAL PROTOCOL: pre-medicate; discontinue vasopressors and remaining non-comfort interventions (not slow titration — the goal is comfort); remove the ventilator (extubation when feasible; otherwise flow-by oxygen for visible comfort while withdrawing pressure support); continue comfort titration
AFTER-DEATH CARE: pronounce per policy; private time at the bedside; mortuary and funeral information; bereavement coordinator; cultural and spiritual practices honored
DOCUMENTATION: substituted-judgment rationale and family-meeting summary; every medication with dose/time/indication; procedure timeline; family interactions; debrief
TEAM SUPPORT: team debrief; acknowledge moral distress; offer chaplaincy and peer support

DO NOT: equate comfort titration with euthanasia; under-medicate from fear of 'hastening' death; abruptly extubate without adequate pre-medication; slowly titrate vasopressors as if survival were the goal; continue non-comfort interventions after the goals have changed; describe comfort medications to the family as 'a lethal injection'; impose the team's values on the surrogate

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters about this end-of-life ICU decision?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Refractory multi-organ failure despite maximal aggressive ICU therapy — the underlying physiology is no longer responsive and continued aggressive care will not meet the patient's stated goals
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the clinical reality that frames the conversation. RN role: recognize the trajectory.

opts.text: An EXECUTED advance directive specifying 'no prolonged life support if no meaningful recovery' and a DPOA-HC who applies SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what the PATIENT would want) anchor the decision
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — values + a documented decision-maker. RN role: anchor in values.

opts.text: WITHDRAWING life-sustaining treatment is ETHICALLY EQUIVALENT to WITHHOLDING it; both are permissible when consistent with the patient's wishes — and DNAR addresses only CPR (comfort care continues across other interventions)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the withholding/withdrawing equivalence and the DNAR distinction. RN role: ethical framework.

opts.text: Compassionate withdrawal with intensive symptom management is NOT euthanasia — under the DOUBLE-EFFECT principle, opioids and benzodiazepines used to relieve suffering are ethically permissible even if they may shorten life
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the central ethical distinction. RN role: communicate this clearly to the team and family.

opts.text: The withdrawal protocol is choreographed — pre-medicate; discontinue vasopressors and non-comfort interventions; remove the ventilator; continue titration to comfort; support family presence; document objectively; debrief
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the procedural plan. RN role: execute the protocol.

opts.text: Family preparation is honest and gentle — variable time to death, irregular and slowing breathing, possible agonal respirations, color changes, secretions, transient eye opening, occasional brief 'rally' — with chaplaincy and bereavement support
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — anticipatory guidance and bereavement. RN role: support family.

opts.text: Under-medicate the patient through the withdrawal because of fear that opioids might 'hasten' death
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Under-medicating violates the duty to relieve suffering; the double-effect principle permits aggressive comfort titration.

opts.text: Describe the comfort medications to the family as 'a lethal injection' or 'a way of ending his life' to be honest with them
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. This conflates compassionate symptom management with euthanasia and inflicts moral injury on the family. Describe the intent and the double-effect principle honestly.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What end-of-life ethics + practice principles apply?

opts.text: Decisions are anchored in the patient's prior expressed values — through an advance directive plus a DPOA-HC applying SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what the patient would want, not what the surrogate would prefer)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the standard. Foundation for honoring autonomy.

opts.text: When prior wishes are unknown, the standard is BEST INTEREST; conflict is managed by clarifying goals, time-limited trials, ethics consultation, and institutional dispute-resolution policies
opts.rat: CORRECT — best-interest and conflict principles. Foundation for difficult cases.

opts.text: Withholding and withdrawing life-sustaining treatment are ethically equivalent
opts.rat: CORRECT — equivalence principle. Foundation for permitting withdrawal.

opts.text: Under the DOUBLE-EFFECT principle, aggressive symptom management with opioids and benzodiazepines at end of life is permissible because the INTENT is relief of suffering — this is NOT euthanasia
opts.rat: CORRECT — double-effect principle. Foundation for aggressive comfort care.

opts.text: Compassionate withdrawal is procedural — pre-medicate adequately, discontinue non-comfort interventions, remove the ventilator (extubation when feasible), and continue titration to comfort; family presence is supported
opts.rat: CORRECT — the protocol principle. Foundation for safe execution.

opts.text: Time to death after withdrawal is variable (minutes to hours, occasionally days) and is shaped by the underlying physiology — share this honestly with the family
opts.rat: CORRECT — honest expectations principle. Foundation for family support.

opts.text: Vasopressors should be slowly titrated DOWN over many hours during compassionate withdrawal because the goal is to preserve survival as long as possible
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The goal at withdrawal is comfort, not survival — vasopressors and other non-comfort interventions are typically discontinued, not slowly titrated; comfort medications are titrated to comfort.

opts.text: DNAR (do-not-attempt-resuscitate) means the patient receives no medical care of any kind and must be transferred to a separate ward
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. DNAR addresses only CPR; other treatments — including aggressive comfort care — continue per plan; no unit transfer is required.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank the priorities for this compassionate withdrawal

opts.text: Confirm the decision is anchored in the patient's values (advance directive + DPOA-HC substituted judgment) and document the goals-of-care change with comfort-focused orders (DNAR + discontinuation of non-comfort interventions)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The ethical and legal foundation must be clear and documented. RN role: anchor in values + document.

opts.text: Prepare the room and family — soft lights, alarms minimized, equipment removed, family at bedside, chaplaincy, honest anticipatory guidance
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. The setting shapes the experience for the patient, family, and team. RN role: prepare.

opts.text: PRE-MEDICATE adequately with an opioid + benzodiazepine bolus BEFORE any maneuver (the most under-done step)
opts.rat: #3 — Adequate pre-medication is the difference between a peaceful and a distressing extubation. RN role: pre-medicate.

opts.text: Discontinue vasopressors and remaining non-comfort interventions, remove the ventilator, and continue titration to comfort with antimuscarinic for secretions
opts.rat: #4 — Execute the structured protocol. RN role: structured withdrawal.

opts.text: Support family presence throughout, deliver after-death care, document objectively, and lead a team debrief
opts.rat: #5 — Closes the experience for family and team. RN role: presence + after-death + documentation + debrief.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this compassionate withdrawal.

opts.text: Anchor the decision in the patient's advance directive and the DPOA-HC's substituted judgment; document the family meeting and rationale; update goals-of-care + DNAR + discontinuation orders
opts.rat: Honors autonomy and meets the ethical-legal foundation. RN role: anchor + document.

opts.text: Prepare the room (soft lights, minimal alarms, equipment removed, patient comfortable), invite the family to the bedside with chaplaincy, and provide honest anticipatory guidance
opts.rat: Sets a humane environment and prepares the family. RN role: room + family preparation.

opts.text: Pre-medicate adequately with an opioid (morphine or fentanyl) and a benzodiazepine (midazolam or lorazepam) BEFORE removing the ventilator; add an antimuscarinic (glycopyrrolate) for secretions
opts.rat: Symptom management under the double-effect principle. RN role: pre-medication.

opts.text: Discontinue vasopressors and remaining non-comfort interventions (not slow titration); remove the ventilator (extubation when feasible) and continue titration to comfort
opts.rat: The structured withdrawal protocol. RN role: execute.

opts.text: Support family presence; reassess comfort every few minutes (face, accessory muscles, grimacing, restlessness, secretions) and re-bolus opioid/benzo as needed; provide after-death care and lead a team debrief
opts.rat: Sustains comfort and supports family and team. RN role: ongoing comfort + after-death + debrief.

opts.text: Under-medicate the patient through the withdrawal because of fear that opioids might 'hasten' death
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — violates the duty to relieve suffering. The double-effect principle permits aggressive comfort titration.

opts.text: Slowly titrate vasopressors down over hours during compassionate withdrawal to preserve survival as long as possible
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the goal is comfort, not survival; vasopressors are typically discontinued, and comfort medications are titrated to comfort.

opts.text: Continue CRRT, routine labs, and other non-comfort interventions because they are already running
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — once goals have changed, non-comfort interventions are stopped; continuing them inflicts burden without benefit.

opts.text: Describe the comfort medications to the family as 'a lethal injection' or as 'ending his life'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — conflates compassionate symptom management with euthanasia and inflicts moral injury on the family. Describe the intent and double-effect honestly.

opts.text: Proceed with withdrawal without a documented surrogate decision and rationale because the team feels the patient 'has suffered enough'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the decision must be anchored in the patient's values via the surrogate or advance directive; the team's emotional assessment alone is not a decision.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm the surrogate's substituted-judgment decision anchored in the advance directive; document the family meeting and update goals-of-care orders (DNAR + discontinuation of non-comfort interventions)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The ethical and legal foundation must be set and documented. RN role: anchor + document.

opts.text: Prepare the room (soft lights, minimal alarms, remove equipment, comfortable position) and invite the family to the bedside with chaplaincy; provide honest anticipatory guidance
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Setting and family preparation shape the experience. RN role: prepare.

opts.text: Pre-medicate adequately with an opioid + benzodiazepine BOLUS before any maneuver (extubation, repositioning) and add an antimuscarinic for secretions
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Pre-medication prevents distress at the most critical moments. RN role: pre-medicate.

opts.text: Discontinue vasopressors and other non-comfort interventions; remove the ventilator (extubation when feasible); continue titration to comfort
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Execute the structured protocol. RN role: structured withdrawal.

opts.text: Continue comfort titration with family presence; pronounce per policy; provide after-death care; document objectively; lead a team debrief
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Comfort + family + after-death + documentation + debrief complete the response. RN role: presence + after-death + debrief.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action/outcome, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The surrogate's decision was anchored in the patient's advance directive via substituted judgment; the family meeting and rationale were documented; goals-of-care orders (DNAR + discontinuation of non-comfort interventions) were updated
opts.rat: Honors autonomy and meets the ethical foundation. Model practice.

opts.text: The room was prepared (soft lights, minimal alarms, equipment removed), the family was at the bedside with chaplaincy, and honest anticipatory guidance was provided
opts.rat: Humane setting + prepared family. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Pre-medication with an opioid + benzodiazepine BOLUS before extubation prevented dyspnea and air hunger; titration to comfort continued throughout; glycopyrrolate addressed secretions
opts.rat: Symptom management under double-effect. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Vasopressors and remaining non-comfort interventions were discontinued; the ventilator was removed; comfort titration continued; the patient died peacefully with his family present
opts.rat: Structured protocol executed humanely. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: After-death care included private time at the bedside, mortuary/funeral information, bereavement coordinator engagement, objective documentation, and a team debrief acknowledging moral distress
opts.rat: Closes the response for family and team. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative — the patient was under-medicated through the withdrawal because the nurse feared opioids might 'hasten' death; he died with visible air hunger and family distress
opts.rat: ⚠️ Under-medicating violates the duty to relieve suffering. The double-effect principle permits aggressive comfort titration.

opts.text: Alternative — vasopressors were slowly titrated down over many hours and CRRT and labs were continued because they were already running; the patient suffered unnecessarily
opts.rat: ⚠️ Once goals changed, non-comfort interventions are stopped — continuing them inflicts burden without benefit.

opts.text: Alternative — the nurse described the comfort medications to the family as 'a lethal injection' and refused to medicate the patient before extubation; the family was traumatized and the patient distressed
opts.rat: ⚠️ Conflates compassionate symptom management with euthanasia and inflicts moral injury. Describe intent and double-effect honestly; pre-medicate adequately.

opts.text: Alternative — the team proceeded with withdrawal without a documented surrogate decision because they felt the patient 'had suffered enough'; documentation was incomplete and the family later questioned the process
opts.rat: ⚠️ Decisions must be anchored in the patient's values via the surrogate or advance directive and documented; the team's feelings alone are not a decision.

opts.text: Alternative — after death the family was rushed out of the room, no bereavement information was offered, and no team debrief was held; moral distress in the team went unaddressed
opts.rat: ⚠️ After-death care for family and team is part of the response; missing it is a failure of the practice, not a logistical convenience.
